key_points: If two chords intersect inside a circle, the products of the segments are equal: AE×EB = CE×ED.
The length of tangent from external point P: PT = √(PA² - r²), where PA = distance from center to P.
Angle between a tangent and a chord through the point of contact equals the angle in the alternate segment.
In a circle, sum of angles subtended by two intersecting chords at the center equals sum of angles in corresponding arcs.
Power of a point theorem: For point outside circle, PA×PB = PC×PD = PT² (tangent), where lines intersect the circle or are tangent.
formulas: Tangent length from external point: PT = √(PA² - r²)
Area of segment: Area of sector - Area of triangle
Chord length from distance d: l = 2√(r² - d²)
examples: Two chords AB and CD intersect at E such that AE=3, EB=5, CE=2. Find ED → AE×EB = CE×ED → 3×5 = 2×ED → ED = 7.5 cm
External point P at 13 cm from center, circle radius 5 cm, tangent length → √(13²-5²)=12 cm
Tangent at point A makes angle with chord AB; angle in alternate segment = ∠opposite arc
Chord of length 8 cm, 3 cm from center, find radius → r = √(d² + (l/2)²)=√(3²+4²)=5 cm
Power of a point: Two secants from external point P intersect circle at A,B and C,D; PA×PB=PC×PD
exam_tips: Use intersecting chord theorem for inside-circle problems.
For tangent-chord problems, remember alternate segment theorem.
Use power of a point theorem for secant-secant, secant-tangent, and tangent-tangent cases.
Always draw diagrams accurately to identify angles and lengths.
For segment areas, subtract triangle area from sector area carefully, especially in radians.
